coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for screening the purine metabolic disorder in human fluids
coredata.dc:creator: Gowthaman, N.S.K.
Sinduja, Bharathi
Karthikeyan, Rangasamy
Rubini, K.
Abraham John, S.
coredata.dc:description: Fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) electrode for the screening of purine metabolic disorder was described in this paper. Peroxynitrite is a short-lived oxidant species that is a potent inducer of cell death. Uric acid (UA) can scavenge the peroxynitrite to avoid the formation of nitrotyrosine, which is formed from the reaction between peroxynitrite and tyrosine (Try). Scavenging the peroxynitrite avoids the inactivation of cellular enzymes and modification of the cytoskeleton. Reduced level of UA decreases the ability of the body from preventing the peroxynitrite toxicity. On the other hand, the abnormal level of UA leads to gout and hyperuricemia. Allopurinol (AP) is administered in UA lowering therapy. Thus, the simultaneous determination of UA, Try and AP using N-CDs modified glassy carbon (GC) electrode was demonstrated for the first time. Initially, N-CDs were prepared from L-asparagine by pyrolysis and characterized by different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The HR-TEM image shows that the average size of the prepared N-CDs was 1.8±0.03nm. Further, the N-CDs were directly attached on GC electrode by simple immersion, follows Micheal's nucleophilic addition. XPS of N-CDs shows a peak at 285.3eV corresponds to the formation of C–N bond. The GC/N-CDs electrode shows higher electrocatalytic activity towards UA, Tyr and AP by not only shifting their oxidation potentials toward less positive potential but also enhanced their oxidation currents in contrast to bare GC electrode. The GC/N-CDs electrode shows the limit of detection of 13×10−10 M (S/N=3) and the sensitivity of 924μAmM−1 cm−2 towards the determination of UA. Finally, the N-CDs modified electrode was utilized for the determination of UA, Tyr and AP in human blood serum and urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
L-asparagine
Purine metabolic disorder
Uric acid
Human fluids

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of N-CDs on GC electrode
Characterization of N-CDs by UV–visible, FT-IR, HR-TEM and spectrofluorimetry
Characterization of N-CDs modified electrode by XPS, SEM and XRD analysis
Electrocatalytic activity of GC/N-CDs electrode towards Tyr and AP
Selective determination of UA in the presence of Tyr and AP
Simultaneous determination of UA in the presence of Tyr and AP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–visible spectra obtained for (a) L-asparagine and (b) N-CDs and (B) HR-TEM image of N-CDs (Inset A: photographs of (a) L-asparagine and (b) N-CDs under UV light).
(A) XPS survey spectrum obtained for N-CDs modified GC substrate. Deconvoluted XPS profile of (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) O1s regions.

(A) CVs obtained for 0.5mM UA at (a) bare GC and (b) GC/N-CDs electrodes in 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. (B) DPVs obtained at GC/N-CDs electrode for each 10μM additions of UA (a-o). Inset: plot of oxidation current vs. concentration of UA.

CVs obtained for 0.5mM (A) Tyr and (B) AP at (a) bare GC and (b) GC/N-CDs electrodes in 0.2M PB solution at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. DPVs obtained for each 10μM addition of (C) Tyr and (D) AP at GC/N-CDs electrode (a–o). Insets: plot of oxidation current vs. concentration of (C) Tyr and (D) AP.

DPVs obtained for (A) each increment of 10μM UA in the presence of 0.5mM each Tyr and AP and (B) each increment of 10μM UA, 20μM Tyr and 30μM AP and Amperometric i-t curves obtained for (C) each 20nM addition of UA and (B) addition of (a) 20nM, (b) 50nM, (c) 100nM, (d) 200nM, (e) 500nM, (f) 1μM, (g) 2μM, (h) 5μM, (i) 10μM, (j) 20μM, (k) 50μM and (l) 100μM of UA at the time interval of 50s at GC/N-CDs electrode in 0.2M PBS (pH 7.2) Eapp=+0.50V. Insets C and D: calibration plots obtained for current vs. concentration of UA.

DPVs obtained at GC/N-CDs electrode in (a) absence and (b) presence of (A) human blood serum and (B) human urine sample, (c) and (d) each addition of 20, 40, 60μM of UA, Tyr and AP in (A) human blood serum and (B) urine sample in PB solution (pH 7.2).

head.ce:title: Fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for screening the purine metabolic disorder in human fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute, Gandhigram 624302, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) electrode for the screening of purine metabolic disorder was described in this paper. Peroxynitrite is a short-lived oxidant species that is a potent inducer of cell death. Uric acid (UA) can scavenge the peroxynitrite to avoid the formation of nitrotyrosine, which is formed from the reaction between peroxynitrite and tyrosine (Try). Scavenging the peroxynitrite avoids the inactivation of cellular enzymes and modification of the cytoskeleton. Reduced level of UA decreases the ability of the body from preventing the peroxynitrite toxicity. On the other hand, the abnormal level of UA leads to gout and hyperuricemia. Allopurinol (AP) is administered in UA lowering therapy. Thus, the simultaneous determination of UA, Try and AP using N-CDs modified glassy carbon (GC) electrode was demonstrated for the first time. Initially, N-CDs were prepared from L-asparagine by pyrolysis and characterized by different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The HR-TEM image shows that the average size of the prepared N-CDs was 1.8±0.03nm. Further, the N-CDs were directly attached on GC electrode by simple immersion, follows Micheal's nucleophilic addition. XPS of N-CDs shows a peak at 285.3eV corresponds to the formation of C–N bond. The GC/N-CDs electrode shows higher electrocatalytic activity towards UA, Tyr and AP by not only shifting their oxidation potentials toward less positive potential but also enhanced their oxidation currents in contrast to bare GC electrode. The GC/N-CDs electrode shows the limit of detection of 13×10−10 M (S/N=3) and the sensitivity of 924μAmM−1 cm−2 towards the determination of UA. Finally, the N-CDs modified electrode was utilized for the determination of UA, Tyr and AP in human blood serum and urine samples.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized and fabricated on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) without any linker. The N-CDs modified electrode is exploited for the simultaneous determination of uric acid (UA), tyrosine (Tyr) and allopurinol (AP) in human fluids.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) (7,9-dihydro-1H purine-2,6,8(3H)-trione) is produced from purines by the enzyme xanthine oxidase via the purine metabolism pathway and it is further degraded by urate oxidase to form allantoin which is freely excreted from the body through urine (Balis, 1974). It is a natural antioxidant, accounting for ∼60% of the free radical scavenging activity and it can scavenge superoxide, hydroxyl radical and singlet oxygen (Hooper et al., 1998). UA may assist in the removal of superoxide by preventing against the degradation of superoxide from cell which in turn blocks the formation of peroxynitrite by preventing its reaction with nitric oxide (NO). Peroxynitrite toxicity results from a number of different mechanisms including the nitration of amino acids such as tyrosine and cysteine and DNA mutations. Besides, breakages resulting from oxidizing modifications that ultimately leads to cell death via necrosis and apoptosis (Beckman, 1996). Tyrosine nitration can lead to the alteration and inactivation of number of enzymes and modifications in the cytoskeletal organization (Kutzing and Firestein, 2008). UA is also very effective by preventing peroxynitrite from nitrating the tyrosine residues of proteins, thereby preventing the inactivation of cellular enzymes and modification of the cytoskeleton (Van Der Veen et al., 1997). But, a reduced UA concentration may decrease the ability of the body to prevent peroxynitrite and free radicals from acting on cellular components and damaging the cell (Hooper et al., 1998). Thus, UA and Tyr are stable markers of peroxynitrite mediated damage and hence, it is essential to monitor the level of UA and Tyr in human fluids. On the other hand, the abnormal level of UA leads to gout (Richette and Bardin, 2010), hyperuricemia (Borghi et al., 2014), Lesch-Nyhan syndrome (Nyhan, 2005) and multiple sclerosis (Toncev et al., 2002). This can be avoided with the co-administration of prophylactic medication along with the UA lowering therapy. Allopurinol (AP) is a well tolerated, inexpensive and commonly used UA lowering agent. It inactivates the enzyme xanthine oxidase which is responsible for the conversion of hypoxanthine to xanthine and xanthine to UA. Hence, simultaneous determination of UA, Tyr and AP is very important in order to avoid the peroxynitrite toxicity besides gout and hyperuricemia.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that comprise discrete nanoparticles with diameters of approximately 10nm (Bourlinos et al., 2008). Xu et al. (2004) accidently discovered CDs during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes. CDs attracted intensive attention in recent years due to their applications in bioimaging (Ray et al., 2009), catalysis (Prasad et al., 2013), biosensors (Shi et al., 2011; Li et al., 2011), dye-sensitized solar cells (Xie et al., 2014) and light emitting devices (Zhang et al., 2013). They are highly fluorescent due to size and quantum confinement effects. Moreover, CDs have excellent water solubility because of their abundant carboxylic acid moieties at the surface and are facile to further modification with various organic, polymeric, inorganic or biological species. Compared to traditional quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes, photoluminescent CDs are superior in terms of robust chemical inertness, tunable emissions, ease of functionalization, low toxicity and excellent physicochemical and photochemical stability. CDs were synthesized by different approaches which include arc-discharge method, laser ablation, ultrasonication, heating, microwave pyrolysis and electrochemical methods (Li et al., 2012; Baker and Baker, 2010). Doping CDs with heteroatom can effectively tune their intrinsic properties including optical characteristics and surface features. Nitrogen, having five valence electrons for bonding with carbon atoms is the most commonly used heteroatom to enhance the properties of CDs (Xu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014). The electronic properties of CDs can drastically alter by doping of the electron-rich nitrogen atom which leads to provide more active sites. Doping of nitrogen not only leads to significant changes in the electronic property but also leads to the enhanced catalytic activity. Further, the surface energy of the system can be reduced by the doped nitrogen atoms ingrained in the lattice and hence the N-doped CDs provide large surface area to adsorb the analyte molecules (Xu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014).

Among the wide variety of analytical techniques for the determination of UA (Ross, 1994; Tan et al., 2014; Martinez-Pérez et al., 2003; Moghadam et al., 2011; Erden and Kilic, 2013) electrochemical methods (Lakshmi et al., 2011) received huge attention because they are less expensive, more selective and sensitive, highly reproducible and less time consumption. Although UA is determined by electrochemical method by different nanomaterials modified electrodes including graphene (Kuila et al., 2011), carbon nanotubes (Jacobs et al., 2010), metal nanoparticles (Saha et al., 2012) and polymer composite modified electrodes (Tiwari et al., 2015), these electrodes involve tedious fabrication procedure, high cost and difficult synthetic methods and lack of sensitivity towards practical application. Hence, an attempt is made to develop a cost effective sensor for screening the purine metabolic disorder in this paper using economically viable N-CDs.

In the present study, the N-CDs were prepared from L-asparagine and characterized by UV–visible, FT-IR spectroscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and spectrofluorimetry. The prepared N-CDs were attached on glassy carbon (GC) electrode and characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. XPS studies showed that N-CDs were attached on GC surface through C-N linkage. The GC/N-CDs electrode was utilized to determine UA, Tyr and AP simultaneously. The practical application of N-CDs modified electrode was demonstrated by determining the concentration of UA in human blood serum and urine samples in the presence of Tyr and AP.

L-asparagine and L-tyrosine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and were used as received. Na2HPO4·2H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O were purchased from Merck, India and were used as received. Uric acid (UA) was purchased from SISCO Research laboratory. All other chemicals used in this investigation were of analytical grade and were used as received. Glassy carbon (GC) plates were purchased from Alfa Aeaser. Indium tin oxide (ITO) plates were purchased from Asahi Beer Optical Ltd., Japan. 0.2M phosphate buffer solution (PB solution, pH 7.2) was prepared using Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. Blood serum samples for practical analysis were received from Lakshmi clinical laboratory, Chinnalapatti, Dindigul.

UV–visible absorption spectra were measured using JASCO V-550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectral measurements were performed on a JASCO FP-8500 spectrofluorometer equipped with xenon discharge lamp. The FT-IR measurements were recorded using Thermo Fisher-Nicolet-iS5 spectrophotometer. HR-TEM images were taken from JEOL JEM 2100, USA operating at 200kV. XRD analysis was carried out with X-ray diffraction unit Rigaku, USA. XPS measurement was carried out using Shimadzu axis 165 high-performance multi-technique analysis. SEM measurements were carried at VEGA3 TESCAN, Czech Republic. The electrochemical measurements were carried out with CHI electrochemical workstation (Model 643B, Austin, TX) and were performed in a conventional two-compartment three electrode cell with a 3mm GC electrode as a working electrode, Pt wire as a counter electrode and NaCl saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode. All the electrochemical measurements were carried under a nitrogen atmosphere at room temperature.

N-doped CDs were prepared by pyrolysis of L-asparagine. Briefly, 0.5g of L-asparagine was heated to 100°C in a heating mantle. It was liquated after 10min and the colourless solution was changed to brown within 20min. The obtained brown colour solution was diluted by adding 50mL of 10mg/mL NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of N-CDs on GC electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The GC electrode was polished with 0.05µm alumina slurry and sonicated in water for 15min to remove the adsorbed alumina particles. The cleaned electrode was then immersed into the colloidal solution of 1mL N-CDs for 8h. The N-CDs were attached on GC electrode through the Michael's nucleophilic addition of amine group on the olefinic bond present on GC electrode (Gallardo et al., 2006). This electrode is termed as GC/N-CDs electrode. For SEM and XPS measurements, GC plate was used as a substrate and for XRD measurement, ITO plate was used as a substrate with similar modification procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CDs by UV–visible, FT-IR, HR-TEM and spectrofluorimetry
ce:section.ce:para: The electronic spectroscopy is one of the simplest techniques to characterize the CDs. The UV–visible absorption spectra recorded for L-asparagine and N-CDs are shown in Fig. 1A. Aqueous solution of L-asparagine does not show any absorption band in the UV–vis region (curve a). On the other hand, the N-CDs show an absorption band at 207nm along with a shoulder band around 307nm (curve b), corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. Fig. 1A inset shows the photographs of L-asparagine and N-CDs under UV irradiation. L-asparagine does not show luminescence under UV illumination (Fig. 1A, inset a) whereas, N-CDs show strong bluish green luminescence (Fig. 1A, inset b) which is attributed from the edge effect and quantum confinement of CDs. The obtained luminescence confirms the successful formation of N-CDs. The FT-IR spectroscopy was used to identify the functional groups present on the surface of N-CDs. FT-IR spectrum obtained for asparagine is shown in Fig. S1A. It shows bands characteristics of C˭O, –NH2 and –OH functional groups at 1648, 3380 and 3440cm−1, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum obtained for liquid N-CDs shows a band at 1634cm−1 due to the C˭O stretching vibration of the amide group and a broad band obtained at 3345cm−1 corresponds to both –NH2 and –OH groups (Fig. S1B). These results suggest that the surface of N-CDs contains hydroxyl and carboxylic groups and –NH2 group. Due to the presence of these hydrophilic groups, the N-CDs had excellent water solubility. The size and the morphology of N-CDs are examined by HR-TEM analysis. The HR-TEM image shows that the N-CDs were roughly spherical in shape (Fig. 1B) with the average size of 1.8±0.03nm (Fig. S2A). The SAED pattern of the N-CDs illustrates the amorphous nature of N-CDs (Fig. S2B). Fig. S3 shows the emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. It can be seen that there is no significant change in the emission maximum while varying the excitation wavelength (Fig. S3). This implies that the size and surface states of N-CDs were uniform. The N-CDs show maximum emission at 441nm when exciting at 340nm (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CDs modified electrode by XPS, SEM and XRD analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The as prepared N-CDs were attached on GC electrode and characterized by XPS, SEM and XRD techniques. Fig. 2A shows the XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs modified GC substrate. It shows peaks around 285, 400 and 532eV corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s region of N-CDs modified substrate. Fig. 2B shows C 1s region of N-CDs modified substrate. It shows peaks at 284.3, 285.3, 286.4 and 288.4eV corresponding to C˭C/C-C, C-N, C-O and C˭O/C˭N bonds, respectively. The N 1s region shows the peaks at 398.6, 399.5 and 400.3eV corresponding to C-N-C, (C)3-N and N-H bonds, respectively (Fig. 2C). Fig. 2D shows the O 1s region for various oxygen functional groups. It shows peaks at 531.7 and 533.3eV corresponding to C-O-C/C-OH and C˭O bonds, respectively. The appearance of a peak at 285.3eV due to C–N bond confirmed the attachment of N-CDs on GC substrate through C-N linkage.

The XRD pattern of N-CDs modified substrate is shown in Fig. S4. The diffraction peaks appeared at 26.30°, 33.39°, 37.70° and 39.56° corresponding to graphitic carbon planes of C (002), C (111), C (020) and C (021), respectively, confirming the successful attachment of N-CDs. The size and morphology of N-CDs modified substrate was characterized by SEM analysis. Fig. S5 shows the SEM image obtained for N-CDs modified GC substrate with different magnifications. The N-CDs were uniformly distributed on the GC surface and the size of the N-CDs was found to be 18nm after attached on the GC surface.

Fig. S6 shows the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) obtained for bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes in the presence of 1mM [Ru[NH3]6]Cl3 in 0.2M PB solution (pH=3) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. The bare GC electrode shows a reversible peak with a peak separation of 70mV corresponding to the [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ redox couple. On the other hand, the N-CDs modified electrode shows a reversible redox peak with a peak separation of 67mV with enhanced current, indicating the efficient pathway provided by N-CDs for electron transfer. The electrochemical surface area of electrode (A) can be calculated from Randles-Sevcik Eq. (1).
(1)
i
p
= 2
.
69
×
1
0
5
n
3 / 2
A
D
1 / 2
C
n
1 / 2
where, ip is maximum current (A), n is number of electrons transferred in redox event (n=1 for Ru (NH3)6 3+/4+ redox probe), A is the electrochemical surface area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient (9.1×10−6 cm2 s−1 for Ru(NH3)6 3+/4+ redox probe), C is the concentration of the redox probe (1mol/cm3) and ν is the scan rate (50mVs−1). The electrochemically active surface area of N-CDs modified electrode was found to be 0.1071cm2 which is almost 1.5 times higher than the geometric area of the bare GC electrode (0.07cm2).

Fig. S7 shows the Nyquist, Bode-phase angle and Bode-amplitude plots for bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes in 1mM Ru(NH3)6Cl3 in 0.2M phosphate buffer solution (pH 3) at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz. Fig. S7A shows the Nyquist plots obtained for bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes and were best fit with Randles equivalent circuit model [Rs(CPE-Rp)] (inset Fig. S7A). The charge transfer resistance (Rct) can be calculated from the semi-circle obtained in the Nyquist plot. Bare GC electrode shows the Rct value of 34.8kΩ whereas the N-CDs modified electrode exhibits 11kΩ. The decrease in Rct value reveals that the electron transfer reaction is more facile at the GC/N-CDs electrode. Fig. S7B shows the Bode-phase angle plot for bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes. It is well known that if the phase angle is greater than or equal to 90°, the electrode behaves like an ideal capacitor, indicating the insulating nature of the electrode (Mcintyre and Pham, 1996). On the other hand, if the phase angle is less than 90° then, the electrode is allowing ions into the surface area of the electrode. Electron transfer reaction was facile through the pinholes or defects in the modified electrode. As shown in Fig. S7B, the phase angle values of both bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes were less than 90° and thus the electrodes did not behave like an ideal capacitor. The GC/N-CDs electrode have less phase angle (68°) when compared to bare GCE (85°). The decrease in the phase angle value for GC/N-CDs electrode suggests that N-CDs act like a conducting channel which facilitates the electron transfer reaction between the redox probe and underlying GC electrode. Fig. S7C shows the Bode-amplitude plots for bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes. At higher frequencies, the values of |Z| are almost constant for both bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes (Fig. S7C). At lower frequencies, the total impedance is equal to the sum of Rs and Rct values. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) was drastically decreased after the attachment of N-CDs on GC electrode. The heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (ket) can be calculated using the following Eq. (2),
(2)
k
e t
=
R T
n
2
F
2
A
R
c t
C
where R is the gas constant, T is the temperature, F is the Faraday constant, A is the area (0.07cm2), Rct is the charge transfer resistance, C is the concentration of redox couple (1mM) and n is the number of electrons transferred (n=1 for Ru(NH3)6 3+/4+ redox couple). The calculated ket values are 1.0×10−4 cms−1 and 3.43×10−4 cms−1 for bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes, respectively. The higher ket value obtained for GC/N-CDs electrode indicates that the electron transfer reaction was faster when compared to bare GC electrode.

Fig. 3A shows the CVs obtained for the oxidation of 0.5mM UA at bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes in 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. The bare GC electrode shows UA oxidation peak at 0.42V (curve a, solid line). After five cycles, the oxidation peak was shifted to more positive potential with decreased current (curve a, dotted line). This may be due to the surface fouling caused by the oxidation of products of UA (Balis, 1974). On the other hand, the GC/N-CDs electrode exhibits UA oxidation at 0.39V with higher current than bare GC electrode (curve b, solid line). The oxidation peak of UA was stable even after five cycles at GC/N-CDs electrode (curve b, dotted line). The higher oxidation current exhibited by GC/N-CDs electrode towards the oxidation of UA is due to higher surface area provided by N-CDs besides the π-π interaction between UA and N-CDs. The obtained results reveal that the N-CDs modified GC electrode is highly suitable for the determination of UA. The effect of pH on the oxidation of UA at GC/N-CDs electrode was studied in the range of 3–10 to optimize the pH for its determination. Fig. S8 shows the CVs obtained for 0.5mM UA at GC/N-CDs electrode from pH 3–10. The oxidation potential of UA was shifted to less positive potential while increasing the pH whereas the oxidation peak current of UA increases from pH 3–7 and then decreases gradually from 7 to 10 (Fig. S8). Hence, the physiological pH (pH 7.2) was chosen for further studies.

Fig. S9 shows CVs obtained for 0.5mM UA at GC/N-CDs electrode in 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2) at different scan rates ranging from 10 to 100mVs−1. While increasing the scan rate, the oxidation peak current was increased linearly. The plot of oxidation peak current versus the square root of the scan rate was linear with a correlation coefficient of 0.9976 (Fig. S9, inset), indicating that the oxidation of UA was controlled by diffusion. Since GC/N-CDs electrode shows a stable response for the oxidation of UA, it was further used for the sensitive determination of UA by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique. Fig. 3B shows the DPVs obtained for each increment of 10µM UA in 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2) at GC/N-CDs electrode. The addition of 10µM UA shows an oxidation peak at 0.34V. Further, each increment of 10µM UA increases the oxidation current linearly without affecting the oxidation potential with a correlation coefficient of 0.9938 (Fig. 3B, inset). The obtained results suggest that the sensitive determination of UA is possible at N-CDs modified GC electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of GC/N-CDs electrode towards Tyr and AP
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4A shows the CVs obtained for the oxidation of 0.5mM Tyr at bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes in 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. The bare GC electrode shows an oxidation peak at 0.80V (curve a, solid line) and it was shifted towards more positive potential with decreased current after five cycles (curve a, dotted line). On the other hand, the GC/N-CDs electrode shows an oxidation peak at 0.72V with higher current (curve b, solid line) than bare GC electrode and it was stable even after five cycles (curve b, dotted line). Fig. S10 shows CVs obtained for 0.5mM of Tyr at GC/N-CDs electrode in 0.2M PB solution at different scan rates. While increasing the scan rate from 10 to 100mVs−1, the oxidation peak current was increased linearly. The plot of oxidation peak current versus the square root of the scan rate was linear with a correlation coefficient of 0.9958 (Fig. S10, inset), indicating the oxidation of Tyr is diffusion controlled process. Fig. 4B shows the electrochemical oxidation of 0.5mM AP at bare GC and GC/N-CDs electrodes in 0.2M PB solution at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. An ill-defined broad oxidation peak was obtained for AP at bare GC electrode (curve a, solid line). After 5 cycles, the oxidation peak was shifted to more positive potential with decreased current (curve a, dotted line). It may be due to the surface fouling effect caused by the oxidation products of AP. On the other hand, the GC/N-CDs electrode shows a well-defined oxidation peak at 1.21V with higher oxidation current when compared to bare GC electrode and it was stable even after five cycles (curve b, dotted line). Fig. S11 shows CVs obtained for 0.5mM of AP at GC/N-CDs electrode in 0.2M PB solution at different scan rates. While increasing the scan rate from 10 to 100mVs−1, the oxidation peak current was increased linearly. The plot of oxidation peak current versus the square root of the scan rate was linear with a correlation coefficient of 0.997 (Fig. S11, inset), indicating the oxidation of AP is diffusion controlled process. Fig. 4C and D show the DPVs obtained for each addition of 10µM Tyr and AP in 0.2M PB solution at GC/N-CDs electrode. The addition of 10µM Tyr shows an oxidation peak at 0.69V (Fig. 4C), whereas, the 10µM addition of AP shows an oxidation peak at 1.12V (Fig. 4C). Further, addition of each increment of 10µM Tyr and AP, the oxidation peak currents of Tyr and AP increased linearly with a correlation coefficient of 0.9984 and 0.9984, respectively (Insets: Fig. 4C and D). The obtained higher oxidation current for Tyr and AP at GC/N-CDs electrode is due to higher surface area provided by N-CDs besides the π-π interaction between Tyr, AP and N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective determination of UA in the presence of Tyr and AP
ce:section.ce:para: The present modified electrode was also exploited for the selective determination of UA in the presence of high concentration of Tyr and AP. Fig. 5A shows the DPVs obtained for 10μM UA in the presence of 500μM Tyr and AP in 0.2M PB solution. A clear voltammetric peak was observed for UA at +0.30V even in the presence of 100-fold higher concentration of Tyr and AP. For each increment of 10μM UA to the solution of 500μM Tyr and AP, the oxidation current of UA was increased. The linear current enhancement was observed while increasing the concentration of UA with a correlation coefficient of 0.9958 (Fig. S12A) without affecting the oxidation peak currents and potentials of Tyr and AP. This result suggested that the N-CDs modified electrode was selective towards the oxidation UA even in the presence of 50-fold higher concentrations of Tyr and AP. In addition, an attempt is also made to selectively determine Tyr and AP in the presence of high concentrations of UA and AP and UA and Tyr, respectively to examine the performance of the electrode. Fig. S13 shows the DPV obtained for 10μM of Tyr in the presence of 500μM UA and AP (Fig. S13A) and 10μM of AP in the presence of 400μM UA and Tyr (Fig. S13B). A well defined oxidation peak was observed for 10μM Tyr and AP in the presence of 50-fold excess of UA and AP and UA and Tyr, respectively which revealed that the detection of low concentration of Tyr and AP is also possible in the presence of high concentrations of other two analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of UA in the presence of Tyr and AP
ce:section.ce:para: Since GC/N-CDs electrode exhibits stable electrochemical response towards UA, Tyr and AP, it was used for the simultaneous determination of them. Fig. 5B shows the DPVs obtained for simultaneous determination of UA, Tyr and AP in 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2). A well defined voltammetric signal was observed for 10µM UA, 20µM Tyr and 30µM AP at 0.29, 0.69 and 1.12V, respectively (curve a). While increasing the concentration of UA from 10µM to 150µM (curve a-o), Tyr from 20µM to 300µM (curve a-o) and AP from 30µM to 450µM (curve a-o), the peak currents of respective analytes were increased linearly with correlation coefficients of 0.9948, 0.9918 and 0.9974, respectively (Fig. S12B–D). The potential differences of 400, 500 and 910mV were obtained for UA-Tyr, Tyr-AP and UA-AP, respectively. The oxidation potentials of UA, Tyr and AP were not affected while increasing their concentrations. The above results suggested that simultaneous determination of UA, Tyr and AP is possible at GC/N-CDs electrode.

The sensitivity of the present modified electrode towards the determination of UA was examined by amperometry. Fig. 5C and D shows the amperometric i–t curve obtained for UA at GC/N-CDs electrode in a homogenously stirred 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2) by applying the constant potential of +0.50V. The GC/N-CDs electrode shows the initial current response due to the addition of 20nM UA. Further addition of 20nM UA in each step with a sample interval of 50s increases the current response of UA and the steady state current response was attained within 3s (Fig. 5C). The dependence of response current with respect to concentration of UA was linear from 20 to 260nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9992 (inset of Fig. 5C). Fig. 5D shows that the amperometric current response increased linearly while increasing UA concentration in the dynamic range of 20×10−9 M to 100×10−6 M with a correlation coefficient of 0.9987 (inset of Fig. 5D) and the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 13×10−10 M (S/N=3). The present modified electrode shows the sensitivity of 924μAmM−1 cm−2 towards the oxidation of UA. The obtained linear range and the detection limit of UA at the GC/N-CDs electrode were compared with the reported modified electrodes (Table S1). It is evident from Table S1 that the present modified electrode showed the lowest LOD (13×10−10 M) and a wide range determination of UA (20×10−9–100×10−6 M) towards UA when compared to the reported papers (Gai et al., 2013; Leobregat et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2016; Wolfgang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016; Dey and Satpati, 2017; Nsabimana et al., 2017; Mohamad et al., 2014).

The determination of UA in the presence of common physiological interferents such as urea, glucose, dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA) and citric acid and common metal ions such as Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+ and NH4 + was studied. Fig. S14 shows the amperometric i–t curve for UA at GC/N-CDs electrode in the presence of several interferences in a homogeneously stirred 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2). The increased initial current response was due to the addition of 50nM UA (curve a) and no change in the current response was observed with the addition of 10μM of each urea, glucose and citric acid (curves b-d) to the same solution. Subsequently, 50nM UA was added to the same 0.2M PB solution, an increase in current response was observed and it is similar to the current response observed for the initial addition of UA. Similarly, the addition of 50μM common ions such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+ and NH4 +(curves e-i) and 0.5μM AA and DA (curves j and k) does not increase the current response. The above results indicate that determination of 50nM UA is possible even in the presence of 100-fold excess of common interferences.

The practical application of the present modified electrode was demonstrated by determining the concentration of UA in human fluids. The standard addition technique was used for the determination of UA in human blood serum and urine samples. The blood serum and urine samples were diluted to 20 and 50 times, respectively with 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2) without any treatment. Fig. 6A and B exhibit the DPVs obtained for blood serum and urine samples in 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2). The blood serum in 0.2M PB solution showed the oxidation peak at 0.32V which may be due to the oxidation of UA. While adding 20µM UA, 40µM Tyr and 60µM AP, two more oxidation peaks were observed at 0.67V and 1.12V in addition to UA oxidation peak at 0.32V, corresponding to the oxidation of Tyr and AP, respectively. While increasing the concentration of these analytes, the oxidation current of the respective analytes also increases without any shift in the oxidation peak potential. The above results strongly suggest that GC/N-CDs electrode is highly suitable for the determination of UA even in the presence of Tyr and AP in human blood serum and urine samples. The obtained results suggest that the level of UA can be monitored in human fluids by the present method. Thus, the diseases caused by excess or low level of UA will be screened easily by the N-CDs modified electrode.

In order to investigate the stability of the GC/N-CDs electrode, the CVs for 0.5mM UA in 0.2M PB solution (pH 7.2) were recorded for every 10min interval. It was found that the oxidation peak current of UA remains same with a relative standard deviation of 1.3 (±0.04)% for 20 times repetitive measurements indicating that this electrode has good repeatability. The GC/N-CDs electrode was kept in PB solution (pH 7.2) at room temperature after the voltammetric measurements. The current response was decreased about 2.8±0.05% in one week and 3.9 (±0.05)% in two weeks. To find out the repeatability of the results, three different GC electrodes were modified with N-CDs and their responses towards 0.5mM UA were recorded by 15 repeated measurements. The peak current obtained in the 15 repeated measurements of three independent electrodes showed a relative standard deviation of 3.65±0.05%, confirming that the results are repeatable. The above results showed that the present modified electrode was very much stable and repeatable towards the determination of UA.

The present work demonstrated a simple, facile and reproducible method for the modification of GC electrode with N-CDs for the determination of UA in human fluids. The N-CDs were prepared by pyrolysis and attached on GC electrode through Michael's nucleophilic addition. The XPS study confirmed that N-CDs were attached on GC electrode through C-N linkage. Further, the GC/N-CDs electrode showed higher electrocatalytic activity towards the oxidation of UA, Tyr and AP when compared to bare GC electrode. Then, the GC/N-CDs electrode was successfully utilized for the simultaneous determination of UA, Tyr and AP with potential differences of 400, 500 and 910mV. The GC/N-CDs electrode exhibited lowest limit of detection of 13×10−10 M and higher sensitivity of 924μAmM−1 cm−2 towards UA determination. The practical application of the present modified electrode was successfully demonstrated by determining the concentration of UA in human blood and urine samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Department of Science and Technology (EMR/2016/002898), New Delhi is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.02.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
